1: The oracle-heading identifies a new historical situation: King Zedekiah's inquiry during the Babylonian siege. The Pashhur here (son of Malkijah) is a different figure from Jeremiah 20's Pashhur (son of Immer) — the name was common. Zedekiah's envoy mission (šelaḥ ʾēlāw, sent to him) represents a sudden change in the king's posture toward the prophet — crisis creates need for prophetic guidance that was formerly rejected.
2: Darash-na vaˁadenu et-YHWH ki Nevuhadretshar melech Bavel nilhem ˁalenu — please seek a word from YHWH for us, for Nebuchadnezzar is making war against us. The request dĕrôš-nāʾ (please inquire) is the formulaic petition for prophetic consultation. Ulai yaˁaseh YHWH ˁittanu kechol nifleˁotav — perhaps YHWH will do for us according to all his wonders (niplĕʾôtāyw). The wonders (peleʾ root) are the Exodus miracles — the miraculous deliverances of the past that the king hopes will be repeated. The word ûlay (perhaps) signals the uncertainty: even the king does not expect the prophet to confirm hope.
3: The terse response formula tomar el-Tsidqiyahu — tell Zedekiah this. Jeremiah does not soften the message for the king's envoys. The oracle that follows inverts every category of Exodus-deliverance the king hoped for.
4: Hafokh et kelei hamillhama asher beyedechem — I am turning back (hōpēḵ, the same root as hāpaḵ/overthrew in Jeremiah 20:16) the weapons in your hands. YHWH will redirect Judah's own defensive weapons inward — turning the siege into a trap rather than a defense. Vaˁasafti otam el-toch haˁir hazze — and I will drive them into the center of this city: the weapons designed to repel the enemy become instruments of Judah's own confinement.
5: Venilhamti ani ˁetkem beyad netuyah uvizroˁa hazaqah uvaf uvehema uvqetsef gadol — I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand (yād nĕṭûyâ) and a mighty arm (zĕrôaʿ ḥăzāqâ). The phrase is the Exodus formula in exact reversal: Deuteronomy 4:34; Jeremiah 5:15; 26:8 describe YHWH delivering Israel from Egypt with outstretched hand and mighty arm. Here the same formula is inverted — YHWH who fought for Israel against Egypt now fights against Israel on behalf of the Babylonian enemy. The theological shock is precise: the very power that created Israel now destroys it.
6: Vehikketi et yoshhvey haˁir hazze gam et-haˁadam veˁet-haˁadama — I will strike down the inhabitants of this city, both man and animal (hāʾādām wĕhabbĕhēmâ). The comprehensive scope of death — human and animal — echoes the Exodus plagues, now directed against Jerusalem rather than Egypt. Bedever gadol yamut — they will die by a terrible plague (deber, the pestilence associated with siege conditions).
7: Ulˁachar ken neˁum YHWH ettem et-Tsidqiyyahu melech Yehuda veˁet-ˁavadav veˁet-haˁam — afterward I will hand over Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, and the surviving people to Nebuchadnezzar. Vehikkam lefi-herev lo yaḥus ˁaleyhem velo yaḥmol velo yeraḥem — he will put them to the sword without pity (yaḥûs), without mercy (yaḥmôl), without compassion (yĕraḥēm). The three negations of compassion-terms are a judicial triple-condemnation of divine relentlessness — the usual attributes of divine mercy are suspended, mirroring Israel's own hardness (cf. Jeremiah 18:12).
8: Veˁel haˁam hazze tomar koh amar YHWH hineni nothen lefanechem et-derekh hahayyim veˁet-derekh hammavet — and to this people say: Thus says YHWH — I am setting before you the way of life (dereḵ haḥayyîm) and the way of death (dereḵ hammāwet). The binary is the Deuteronomic choice of Jeremiah 30:15,19 — YHWH sets before Israel life and death, blessing and curse, and commands them to choose life. In the siege context, the choice is given urgently material form: stay and die, or surrender and live. The theological principle from Deuteronomy is enacted in the crisis of 586 BCE.
9: Hayoshev baˁir hazze yamut beherev vebaraˁav uvadaver — whoever stays in this city will die by the sword, famine, and pestilence. Vehayotse venafal ˁal-haKasdim hammatsrim ˁalechem haˁir hahu yihhye vehayeta nafsho lo leshallal — but whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans besieging you will live: vehayeta nafsho lo leshallal — his life will be his prize/plunder (šālāl). Nefesh leshallal (life as plunder) is a unique idiom: in warfare, šālāl is the booty captured in battle; applying it to one's own life means: life stripped of all possessions, status, and dignity — bare existence as the prize. The same idiom recurs in Jeremiah 38:2; Jeremiah 39:18; Jeremiah 45:5.
10: Ki-samti fanai baˁir hazze leraˁa velo letova — for I have set my face against this city for disaster and not for good. Šamtî pānay (I have set my face) — the divine gaze is a covenantal image: the face that shines on the blessed (Numbers 6:25-26) is now fixedly directed toward Jerusalem in judgment. Lĕrāˁâ wĕlōʾ lĕṭôḇâ — for disaster and not for good: the binary of Jeremiah's conditional theology (Jeremiah 18:7-10) has reached its verdict for this generation.
11: The transition to the next oracle section (wĕlebet melech Yehuda, and to the house of the king of Judah) is addressed to the royal household specifically — the ruling class bears the first responsibility for the covenant's administration of justice.
12: Beit David koh amar YHWH dinu laboqer mishpat — House of David! Administer justice every morning (labbōqer, for the morning). The morning-time obligation for justice reflects ancient Near Eastern judicial practice: the city gate courts met in the morning when heat was manageable and commerce was beginning. Vehatsillu gazul miyad ˁosheq — rescue the robbed from the oppressor (ʾôšēq): the royal function of protecting the vulnerable from exploitation is the central covenant justice-obligation. Pen-tetse kha-esh ḥamati — lest my wrath go out like fire: the unquenchable fire is the divine judgment that even the Davidic dynasty cannot escape if it fails the justice-obligation.
13: Hineni ˁaleyich yoshhevet haˁemeq tsur hamishor neˁum YHWH — I am against you, enthroned in the valley on your rocky plateau. Jerusalem's geography becomes ironically its indictment: the city trusted its physical invulnerability (the valley defenses and the rocky plateau of the Ophel ridge) as security against invasion. Haaomrim mi-yered ˁaleynu umi-yavo bimskenoteinu — who say, Who can attack us? Who can penetrate our strongholds? The rhetorical confidence is the voice of false security rooted in topography rather than covenant fidelity.
14: Vefaqadti ˁalechem kifri maˁalleichem neˁum YHWH — I will punish you according to the fruit (pĕrî) of your deeds. The agricultural metaphor for moral consequence: deeds have a harvest; what has been planted in injustice will be reaped in judgment. Vehitsatti esh beyaˁarach vaʾaklah kol-sevivoteha — I will kindle fire in its forest and it will consume everything around it: the city that is surrounded by forest (the forests of Lebanon's timber and Judah's wooded hills) will be consumed from within by its own judgment-fire.
